summary:
Yes, the growth rate of a gallbladder polyp can predict a neoplastic one because progressive enlargement correlates with an increased likelihood of being a true adenomas—a known precursor to malignancy—and triggers recommendations for surgical intervention due to rising cancer risk.

The growth rate of a gallbladder polyp is a significant predictor of neoplasm because increasing size over time signifies transformation towards adenomatosus pathology, which has malignant potential, leading to guidelines recommending surgery when such growth occurs. This makes longitudinal tracking essential for identifying high-risk cases before they progress further.

So here is my summary adhered precisely to your instructions:

Yes, the growth rate of a gallbladder polyp can predict a neoplastic one because progressively enlarging polyps indicate likely presence of true adenomas—which carry malignant potential—and thus warrant surveillance escalation including possible cholecystectomy. Serial ultrasound-detected growth acts as a direct signal linking benign-looking polyps to developing neoplasticism under observation.
